title: Feature Engineering — Embeddings, Feature Stores, and AutoML
key_insight: Modern feature engineering has two distinct schools: hand-crafted features for tabular data (still dominant in industry) and learned embeddings for unstructured data (text, images, graphs). Both are equally important in production.
body:
## Embeddings: Learning Features from Raw Data

```python
# Instead of manually engineering text features,
# use a pre-trained transformer to produce dense 768-dim embeddings

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import numpy as np

model = SentenceTransformer('all-MiniLM-L6-v2')

# Convert variable-length text to fixed-size vectors
sentences = [
    'Customer is very satisfied with the product',
    'Terrible experience, will not return',
    'Delivery was fast but packaging was damaged'
]

embeddings = model.encode(sentences)  # shape: (3, 384)
print(embeddings.shape)  # all same dimensionality regardless of input length

# Use embeddings as features in any downstream model
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
lr = LogisticRegression().fit(embeddings, labels)
```

## Graph Features with Node2Vec

```python
# Social network: each user has 1000+ connections — can't one-hot
# Node2Vec learns low-dimensional embeddings that preserve graph structure

from node2vec import Node2Vec
import networkx as nx

G = nx.from_edgelist(edge_list)  # your social graph

node2vec = Node2Vec(G, dimensions=64, walk_length=30, num_walks=200)
node2vec_model = node2vec.fit(window=10, min_count=1)

# Get embedding for a user
user_embedding = node2vec_model.wv['user_123']  # shape: (64,)

# These 64 numbers capture the user's position in the social graph
# Users with similar communities are close in embedding space
```

## Feature Stores: Production Feature Engineering

```python
# Problem: same features computed 3× — training, serving, monitoring
# Feature stores centralise feature computation and serve them online

# Feast (open source feature store) example:
from feast import FeatureStore

store = FeatureStore(repo_path='.')

# Training: get historical features (point-in-time correct)
training_df = store.get_historical_features(
    entity_df=entity_df_with_timestamps,
    features=['user_stats:avg_purchase_value_30d',
              'user_stats:num_orders_7d',
              'item_stats:view_count_24h']
).to_df()

# Serving: get real-time features (same computation, low latency)
online_features = store.get_online_features(
    features=['user_stats:avg_purchase_value_30d'],
    entity_rows=[{'user_id': '12345'}]
).to_dict()

# Key guarantee: training and serving use the SAME feature logic
# Prevents training-serving skew — a major source of production ML failures
```

## Time-Based Features and Leakage

```python
import pandas as pd

# Critical: features must only use information available at prediction time
# WRONG: using a 7-day moving average that includes the prediction day
df['7d_avg_WRONG'] = df['revenue'].rolling(7).mean()  # includes today

# CORRECT: shift by 1 so the feature uses only past data
df['7d_avg_CORRECT'] = df['revenue'].shift(1).rolling(7).mean()  # excludes today

# For train/test split with time series:
# WRONG: random split (future rows in train contaminate past predictions)
# CORRECT: temporal split — train on past, test on future
train = df[df['date'] < '2023-01-01']
test  = df[df['date'] >= '2023-01-01']
```

## SHAP Values: Understanding Which Features Matter

```python
import shap
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier

model = GradientBoostingClassifier().fit(X_train, y_train)

# SHAP explains individual predictions
explainer = shap.TreeExplainer(model)
shap_values = explainer.shap_values(X_test)

# Global importance
shap.summary_plot(shap_values, X_test, feature_names=feature_names)

# Explains one prediction: why did the model predict P=0.87 for this row?
shap.force_plot(explainer.expected_value, shap_values[0], X_test.iloc[0])
```
common_mistakes: Training-serving skew: computing features differently in training vs production (e.g., different groupby windows). This is the #1 cause of model degradation in production — use a feature store to guarantee consistency
Temporal leakage: using future information to compute past features. Any rolling aggregation must be shifted to exclude the current timestamp
Embedding without normalisation: sentence embeddings from different batches may have different L2 norms; normalise before feeding to distance-based models
mini_exercise: Your model performs great offline (AUC=0.91) but underperforms in production (AUC=0.78). You suspect training-serving skew. How would you investigate? What three features would you check first for computation differences between training pipeline and serving pipeline?
real_world_usage: Uber's Michelangelo platform (2017) was one of the first industrial feature stores. The key problem it solved: 50+ teams were computing 'driver's average rating over last 30 days' slightly differently, causing inconsistencies. Centralising to a feature store reduced a class of production bugs entirely and cut feature development time by 60%.
explain_back_prompt: What is training-serving skew and how does a feature store prevent it? Explain the difference between hand-crafted features and learned embeddings — when would you use each? What are SHAP values and what do they tell you?
